questions.question: What is the core mistake in treating rebalancing as only a background copy job?
options: It ignores that ownership, routing, and correctness are changing while live traffic continues.
It assumes copy jobs are always slower than databases.
It guarantees that no rollback is ever needed.
It means capacity planning becomes unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Rebalancing affects the serving path, so routing, writes, and rollback all matter.

questions.question: Why should a rebalance usually move data in bounded batches?
options: To limit blast radius and protect serving capacity if something goes wrong.
Because distributed systems cannot copy more than one range at once.
Because small batches remove the need for verification.
Because cutover metadata cannot store multiple moves.
questions.explanation: Bounded concurrency is a direct blast-radius control.

questions.question: What is the safest high-level sequence for live data movement under active writes?
options: Copy, keep up with deltas, verify, switch routing epoch, then retire the source.
Flip routing first, then start copying, and verify only if alarms fire later.
Delete the source as soon as the target starts receiving bytes.
Pause all observability during the move to reduce overhead.
questions.explanation: Safe moves preserve the source until the target is current and verified.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of keeping the source owner available after copy completes?
options: You preserve rollback safety, but temporarily pay extra capacity and operational complexity.
You eliminate all routing-version concerns automatically.
You make dual-write impossible.
You guarantee the move finishes faster.
questions.explanation: Safe rollback paths cost extra resources, but they are worth it for correctness.

questions.question: Which metric best warns that rebalance activity is harming the serving path?
options: Serving latency and error rate correlated with active move count or copy bandwidth.
The number of lines in the migration playbook.
The size of the developers' local Git history.
The total count of feature flags in the company.
questions.explanation: The key question is whether copy activity is consuming too much serving headroom.

questions.question: Why is versioned routing or ownership epochs important during cutover?
options: They help clients and services detect stale ownership and redirect safely instead of silently misrouting.
They remove the need for target verification entirely.
They ensure all writes become globally ordered forever.
They make bandwidth planning irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Epochs make stale views visible and recoverable during ownership changes.

questions.question: What is the real danger of launching too many moves in parallel?
options: Copy traffic, warmup misses, and control-plane churn can combine into a serving outage.
The system will stop needing rebalancing in the future.
It permanently disables replication.
It makes data smaller so checksums fail.
questions.explanation: Parallel moves raise multiple risks at once, which is why batch limits matter.

questions.question: When might a short write freeze be an acceptable migration strategy?
options: When the moved scope is small, business tolerance is explicit, and dual-write complexity is not justified.
Whenever the engineering team wants the simplest code regardless of customer impact.
Only when the data is already corrupted.
Never, because all migrations must be completely invisible.
questions.explanation: A short freeze can be reasonable for tightly scoped moves when its cost is understood and acceptable.
